title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Daniel Beutel: Flower SuperGrid Agents
transcript: I'm here today to talk about a new frontier that we see opening in AI. That frontier is collaborative AI systems. Um that frontier is going to unlock a new category of systems to be built, systems that were not possible before. But before I do that, I just want to say a few words about um who we are at Flower Labs and what we're working on. So, we've had um a truly remarkable year. We've actually um started a year by making history. So, what you can see here is a satellite. It's a satellite in orbit and it's called StarCloud 1. On board of that satellite, you have the first H100 GPU that's ever been run in space. And that GPU um among other things it runs Flower. And so we worked with the amazing team at StarCloud and together we um made history on StarCloud one together with the StarCloud team. We performed the first vision transformer training um in space and that was a quite a nice milestone for us obviously. So that means if anyone of you has contributed to the Flower open source project in the past, there's a good chance that your code actually ran in space. Let's just take another look at this uh for a second. I think it's uh beautiful. And so a little bit about us. Um our story starts in 2022 at the University of Cambridge in the machine learning systems lab. We were in the machine learning systems lab. We were wondering what are things that we can directions that we can work on that have the possibility to change really change the course of where AI is going. Not just something that improves a benchmark by 1% but really something that can have a tremendous um impact. And so we started to build this. This is our logo. At the time, centralized AI was the um predominant way to build AI systems. Centralized AI basically means move the data to the computation. So um you collect as much data as you can in a single place. You build a big GPU cluster and then you train a model. We started to work on decentralized AI approaches which means basically just flip the script, move the computation to the data, let the data stay where it is and leverage distributed compute clusters and distributed data sources in a single network in a single um training run. And so we actually don't like to call it decentralized AI that much. We often call it collaborative AI because it's really this collaborative approach that allows different parties to come together to jointly use compute resources and data resources. In the space of collaborative AI, Flower has um kind of become the industry standard. It's open source under Apache 2.0 license. Developers quite like the framework. Uh we have more than 7,000 people on the public Slack. We have close to 7,000 stars, 180 contributors, and we run quite a few events. The Flower AI Summit has become the world's largest conference for federated AI systems. It's used by large organizations across very different industries. Uh in industry, there's users like um Bosch and Oaken, for example. In academia, all of the top research institutions use Flower. and the consumer space brands like Samsung and Mozilla use FL and then there's other users like the hyperscalers but also the department of energy or the US air force that use flour with this tailwind uh in 2023 we started flower labs as a startup we went into Y combinator in the winter 23 batch and then in 24 we um released two courses actually on deep learning And so you might wonder why collaborative AI? What's the um what's the reason for you to be so interested in that? Our our core motivation is to build and enable others to build better AI systems. And to build better AI systems, the recipe hasn't really changed that much. The basic recipe is you need a better and bigger model. You need more compute to train a better model. and you need more data to train a better model. And that's true, it's easy to forget, but that's true for both the training side of things, but also the infant side of things. So in training, of course, you um want to train a bigger model. On the inference side, the model weights are somewhat fixed, but when you do inference, um you want to spend more time on reasoning and you also want more data in your context window to um enable the model to come up with um better responses. And so one interesting thing I often come back to is this what you see here on the left hand side, that dot represents 15 trillion tokens. those 15 trillion tokens is pretty much all of the high quality English data that you can find on the web. So this is the basis for pretty much all of foundation model training and this is also the data that um agents can access through web search. Now, maybe if you've seen this before, um please no spoilers, but maybe take a guess. How much more data is there in data silos that are not public? Maybe everyone can just take a guess. It's that much. There's another 2,000 trillion tokens that are barely used today. Some of it is used, but um the vast majority of that data is not used today. And so we actually have to zoom out to see the full extent of this. What you see on the left hand side again this is the data that's used and what you see on the right hand side that's um mostly I would say unused. So the factor here is 133x. We're using less than 1% of the data we have in the world. And that factor is actually a very conservative estimate. There are other estimates that make the dot on the right hand side way bigger than that. But of course, reality doesn't really look like this. Reality looks a little bit different. Reality looks a lot more like this with probably a lot more dots on the right hand side. Um, all of this data is distributed across a large number of data silos of very different sizes. And so, how are organizations solving this today? How are organizations um getting access to how do organizations get access to more data? Well, the current approach and I hinted at this before is to basically make your note bigger and you make your note bigger by collecting more data. You make your note bigger by um doing licensing deals with uh other organizations. But it's also fairly obvious that this approach is quite limited because there's only so many licensing deals you can do. There's only so much data that you can collect and there's always going to be a lot more data in these other silos. But there is a different way and that way is to actually build a network. A network allows you to scale out instead of scaling up. So, it's kind of the horizontal scaling versus vertical scaling. It's a new paradigm and to some extent it levels the playing field because it allows new players to emerge. If you're better and faster at building a network, you can you can train models that no one else can train. And so maybe just focus on these uh small dots on the top. Yeah, on the top. Um, maybe those are companies that are maybe not AI native, but they're uh tech companies and they have some some interesting data. If they're really fast at building a network and working together, they can create a hugely valuable resource and on top of that valuable resource, they can train models that no one else can train and they can become competitive in a way that um no one else can compete with. And so it's probably not surprising that our take is that the new frontier is collaboration. We're going from this phase of isolation, building a bigger bigger data silo to a phase of collaboration where these collaborative networks enable us to train better models and to build better agentic AI systems. And so um Andrew famously said AI is the new electricity. And I couldn't agree more for for many different reasons. But I think one one interesting aspect of this quote is if you look at the history of electricity actually electricity also started out as um isolated sidos. If you needed electricity for your factory you had to build a generator right next to it. But then as we all know electricity evolved. It evolved into this collaborative grid where production of electricity and consumption is completely decoupled and a huge unlock actually came from building that network um because it allows all the participants to seamlessly transact. In computing it's very similar. Computing started out with isolated mainframes and eventually through open standards we moved into this collaborative network. The internet personal computing um all gave rise to this uh collaborative network and so that is why we say um the next frontier is going to be collaboration. Once you understand the huge unlock that you can get from this, there's just no way um to unsee this. I think this changes what an AI lab has to be over the next decade. And the next chapter of AI, it will not be built um in isolation. I think the next chapter of AI will be built in these organizational silos. But then by connecting with others and by building out these networks, by bridging these gaps, we'll see AI systems um that we haven't seen today. And so I think it's not enough to build the infrastructure, it's not enough to train models, it's not enough to build agents. I think actually you need to have um all three. That's why our mission is to advance collaborative uh super intelligence because we're building these three building blocks. The three building blocks are you need a decentralized AI platform to run any kind of AI workload on decentralized data. You want to have an agent that is built with collaborate collaboration in mind. So agents that can collaborate. And then you also if you want to improve these agents on the local data, you want to have a decentralized training pipeline. And I'm going to talk about all of these three building blocks um in some more detail now. So the first component that we have is a decentralized AI um platform. That's the first building block. It's called FAR Super Grid. Um if you've ever tried to build a decentralized AI system before, you've probably had to deal um with something like this. So there's a long list of things that you need to do. I think this is over 80 steps. If you go through all of these 80 steps, um you're actually not done. There's another list of more than 80 steps that you need to follow to start building that system to onboard other partners um to make it work. And once you do these steps, guess what? You're still not done. there's another list of steps that you need to follow. And once you followed all of these steps, you're pretty close to being done. You're not done yet, but you're pretty close. And so we wanted to simplify that down into something that is um a lot more doable and that is a lot more approachable. And so that's why we built Super Grid. We asked ourselves, could we make this at least an order of magnitude easier to build such a system? And we said if we do this then we really need to aim for this step change. Can we leaprog whatever is out there and um make it a lot easier and make it a lot more um accessible basically. And so we had to innovate on all layers of the stack to do this. We had to rearchitect what it means um to build a collaborative AI system from scratch. And we ended up with a user experience where it's actually quite easy to forget um how much complexity is going on under the hood. Um because now you can simply go to FL.AI, you can create a new federation. You um give it a name and then you create the federation and that federation allows you to easily collaborate with others. um you can go in there, you can see all the members that it has. So initially um it's just you but then you can easily just like you create a new project on GitHub, you can add new users, you can invite other users and you can start to work with them. The key thing is um super nodes that's what Flower calls the individual nodes that are running on those data silos. you can add individual super nodes and then by adding these super nodes um you have everything there that you need to run a decentralized and distributed AI workloads. So you can go into the terminal. You can then do um a simple command flower run. You tell it um where to run, which federation to use and then those workloads can take advantage of the entire network that you've built up um with these super nodes. So here we we see the run starting, we see um the run ID and then if we go back to all the runs that are happening, we see that this run is running. There's a train running uh a training run happening now and you can of course you can see all the details you can interact with it you can stop it um you can see the logs and that's all it takes to actually um build a decentralized AI systems. It's not these 250 steps anymore. It's just a few clicks and um you're good to go. And so that's super grid um our foundational layer to build decentralized AI systems. It's built on years of R&D. Uh there's a very strong isolation model between the different components. There is auditable component um communication between different components, support for of course um streaming very large model weights for LM training. There's a decentralized trust and governance system. And we also recently introduced the world's first um heterogeneous confidential compute um integration which means that you can use confidential compute from different vendors in the very same system which is a a key requirement to scale out these workloads. There's also flower hub where you can get apps that um have been built by the community for the community with a decentralized um trust and review system. And so that's just a small glimpse at um what Flower Super Grid is. It allows you to build these uh globally distributed AI systems. What I like most is uh what our users actually do with it. Um so one um one of the examples I love is this story from Dr. Nicholas Con at Dockport. They deployed Super Grid in one critical area in primary care. And so they used Super Grid on the data of more than 250,000 patients without ever having to centralize that data. And so in this example, collaborative AI directly helps to improve primary care. If you're interested in working with us on a um decentralized AI system, we have the Flower pilot program. Um, and here's a QR code that you can use to um, apply to the pilot program. So, that's a quick peek at Flower Super Grid, our um, decentralized AI platform, and it's the first building block um, in our three layers. The second layer, the second building block is a collaborative AI agent. And that's the that's the core part of my talk. If we just go back to um this graphic real quick, if you think about an agentic AI system today, most agents can use all the public data um in their context because they have access to web search. The big dot on the right is pretty much all um missing context. Some agents that are more advanced, they have access to um part of that missing context. So if you run an agent in your organization and you give it access to the private data within your organization um then your agent has um access to part of that. But maybe the answer is somewhere else. Maybe the answer is in uh someone else's data. And so the question is what do you do? Um how can you how can you build an agentic system that actually allows you um to get the answer you want? And so fundamentally if we look at the capabilities that an agentic AI system needs there's sort of these three core capabilities. One is the capability to uh retrieve information do a web search or read from private data sources. The second one is the ability to reason over that data and then maybe go back and retrieve more data. And the third capability is to take an action either reply to the user or send an email or take an action in some system. And so the question is what's missing with these three capabilities? It's a little bit like a human sitting alone in a room and working on a task. This is not how how real progress is made. The missing component is of course um collaboration. You need different agents that can collaborate either within the same organization or across different organizations. Collaboration requires messages. It requires one agent to be able to message another agent and then also to get responses from that agent. But actually, if you want to run this in production, it requires a lot more. It requires a governance layer with full auditability with discovery with security with permissioning and quite a bit more. And so to summarize, I think what it requires is uh exactly what we've built with super grid. So how do agents actually run on super grid? How do these um collaborative workloads actually um work? So the super grid architecture is um is fairly simple. If you have multiple organizations, in this case, uh in this example, we just have four organizations, but this could also be four data silos in the in the same organization. Each of these organizations has some kind of a of a data set. And so what you have in super grid is then one coordinator. That coordinator doesn't have any data. Only the organizations um have data. And in Flower, we call that coordinator the superlink. and the individual um nodes we call them super nodes. And so eventually you want to have agents running um on the coordinator side and also on the super node side. And the way this evolves is uh in the beginning super is idle nothing happens. The first step is to trigger the agent to trigger the coordinating agent. that agent breaks down the task and says, "How do I want to use my network and how do I what do I want the individual agents to do?" And so it sends messages to these individual agents. But then maybe one of the agents um one of the super nodes refuses to um take the message which is completely fine. Individual super nodes um they are fully autonomous. They can decide whether they want to participate in the workload or not. Once the message is accepted, the three sup super nodes that accepted the message, they process the message, they work on the message and they have access to their local data and then they send back a message to the um to the central coordinating agent. But maybe one of the super nodes, maybe there's some sensitive data that it came up with and the operator of that super node says, "Oh, this is something I actually don't want it to go out." So they can reject um that message from being sent. And so then um actually just two messages get back to the central agent. The central agent then processes that message, aggregates the results and um returns the results back to the user. And so I think the gist of this is that by working together these agents can solve tasks that none of the agents on their own could just solve um individually. The basic uh philosophy is data never moves. um only agents send messages according to the governance principles that um the super node operators um have configured. And so we built one of these agents uh we call it project Kaya. It's a collaborative AI agent and that agent of course is uh running on super grid. And here's a um quick demo. So this starts out looking like a regular um agentic AI system um in this case prompted by the user. it starts to work but then it's aware that um it has access to this network and so it goes out to the network and you see in this network there's um super nodes connected in San Francisco in Mumbai in Sydney in Seol and so the central agent sends the these messages to these individual super nodes they then start to process there's a few other super nodes that are um disconnected or that refuse the message which is perfectly fine um these individual entreprenees all of the results and then comes back um with a response based on uh those replies that it got from the network and that's it. The run is completed. So that's project Kaya. We have project Kaya in early access. If you're interested in working with us on this um again um you can apply to the flower pilot program or just go to flower.ai and uh reach out to us. So this is the second building block um project kaya a collaborative AI agent. The third building block, if you once you have these decentralized agents running and these decentralized agents um start to collect um agent traces in different places, you need a decentralized training pipeline to train your language models or to train other models um on that data. And so at Flower, we have um years of experience in decentralized trading research. We've published at some of the best conferences um for example we published at ICLR um in 2025 decoupled embeddings for pre-training of language models and other advances such as Photon for um federated pre-training of LMS. Just two weeks ago we announced um our first openweight model. It's called Lizzy 7B. It's a specific model. We wanted to build um an interesting model that um on the one hand generalizes well but on the other hand also has a unique capability and so what we did is we built a model specifically for the UK that is aware of all the intricacies of the UK and so we have a model that u performs really well across other models um in that size category but then especially if you're in the UK it gives you some um it gives you way better answers um compared to of the other models out there. Lizzy 7B is um is on hugging face and I think the interesting thing is that Lizzy 7B was built on Supergrid Frontier. Supergrid Frontier is our training pipeline, our decentralized training pipeline for building these models. In um last year, we announced um one of the results we got with this training pipeline is a up to a factor of 1,000x um reduction in communication cost um over these uh decentralized training runs. And so real quick, I just want to show how a decentralized uh training run would work. It's very similar to the to the agentic run, but um just to show it um one time. So we again we start with an idle super grid. We initialize the model or we take a pre-trained model. We we use an existing checkpoint on the um coordinator side. We sent that model out to the individual participating uh super nodes. These individual super nodes then perform local training using their local data. They send the updated models um back to the coordinator. The coordinator then ends up with um in this case four different updated models, four slightly different models. Those models have seen all the local data and then the coordinator aggregates these models into into one model. There's a whole body of research on how to aggregate these models. We have different um approaches for that built into flower. And basically what you do is you repeat this over and over again. So you send the model out, you distribute the model, uh you run local training, you send back the results, and then you aggregate this again. And so fundamentally what you end up with is a model on the server side that has seen all the local data without the data actually ever having to um move without the data ever having to leave those premises. Um so all this data stays private. And so here the motto is uh data never moves. uh only the models move, only the learnings move and so we're iterating on super good frontier on this training pipeline uh with some of our amazing partners. So one of our amazing partners is the department of energy together with the department of energy Sunday national labs um we are training a 70 billion parameter LLM decentralized across three different uh sites. So that's a sort of a super quick uh preview of what Supergrid Frontier is. It's Flowers decentralized training pipeline.
